available: current Caddy, Smallstep, Let’s Encrypt, and ACME primary documentation
implementation and adversarial evidence created in this run

background.discipline: lighthouse operations and maritime navigation safety
background.experience: lamp redundancy, visibility failures, chart corrections, and handoff logs
background.attention_bias: continuous external trust signal and recovery before darkness
background.counter_bias: rejects a working lamp as proof that the next watch can relight it

readers.thesis: TLS authority survives only when another operator can renew or replace trust before the public signal expires.
observations: The architecture repeatedly separates provider operation from canonical authority.
uncertainties: Production DNS registrar, CA account, and hardware custody have not been exercised in this run.

background.experience: issuer marks, chain-of-custody, counterfeit detection, and authority transitions
background.attention_bias: issuer lineage, key custody, and verification-chain continuity
background.counter_bias: rejects possession of a certificate as ownership of the issuing authority

readers.thesis: The durable object is the trust graph binding names, keys, issuers, account credentials, and recovery evidence—not the current certificate file.
observations: The architecture repeatedly separates provider operation from canonical authority.
uncertainties: Production DNS registrar, CA account, and hardware custody have not been exercised in this run.

agreements: Test renewal and issuer replacement before certificates approach expiry.
Keep public-CA account keys and internal root authority under project custody.
disagreements: Reader A prioritizes operational continuity; Reader B prioritizes issuer and key provenance.
omissions_detected: The first design used DNS-01 with credentials that could modify the complete production DNS zone.
revisions_after_discussion: Delegate the ACME challenge zone and restrict credentials to that zone only.

notes_added: Treat certificate renewal as a recurring release-authority exercise.
Treat the TLS trust graph—not one certificate—as the recoverable identity object.
notes_challenged: Automatic HTTPS alone proves certificate sovereignty.

privacy.review: Contains synthetic technical abstractions and explicit corpus limits only.
